1 tablespoon cooking oil (mustard oil preferred)
2 inches fresh ginger, pounded or finely minced
6-8 dried whole red chilies (adjust to spice preference)
1 teaspoon salt (or to taste)
3 cups water
Fresh coriander leaves, for garnish (optional)
instructions:
1. Heat the oil in a heavy-bottomed pan or Dutch oven over medium-high heat. Add the pork cubes and sear them until they are lightly browned on all sides, rendering some fat (about 5-7 minutes).
2. Reduce the heat to medium. Add the pounded ginger and the whole dried red chilies. Sauté for 2 minutes until the ginger is fragrant, ensuring the chilies do not burn.
3. Add the prepared Daikon radish slices and the salt. Toss well to coat the radish and pork with the spices and rendered fat.
4. Pour in the 3 cups of water (or enough to just cover the ingredients). Bring the mixture to a strong boil, then immediately reduce the heat to low, cover the pan, and let it simmer gently.
5. Cook for 45-60 minutes, or until the pork is fork-tender and the radish is soft but still holds its shape. The stew should be thick and slightly reduced.
6. Taste and adjust salt if necessary. Serve Phagshapa hot with steamed rice or Tingmo (steamed bread), garnished with fresh coriander if desired.

title: Chikhvi (Tripura Bamboo Shoot and Berma Stir-Fry)
ingredients:
500g fresh or canned tender Bamboo Shoots, sliced thinly
50g Berma (Fermented Dried Fish, preferably Hilsa or Puti variety)
3 tbsp Mustard Oil (Sarson ka Tel)
1 medium Onion, thinly sliced (optional)
1 tbsp Ginger, freshly grated or finely julienned
6 cloves Garlic, crushed or roughly chopped
4-6 Green Chilies (Dhania Mirchi), slit lengthwise (adjust to heat preference)
1/2 tsp Turmeric Powder (Haldi)
Salt, to taste (use sparingly, as Berma is naturally salty)
1/2 cup Water
Fresh Coriander leaves, for garnish
instructions:
1. If using fresh bamboo shoots, boil them in water for 10-15 minutes, drain, and rinse thoroughly to remove any bitterness; if using canned shoots, rinse well under cold water.
2. Prepare the Berma (dried fish): If using whole pieces, wash them lightly and mash or chop them roughly. If using Berma paste, measure the quantity.
3. Heat the Mustard Oil in a heavy-bottomed wok (kadhai) until it shimmers and just begins to smoke.
4. Add the crushed garlic, ginger, and green chilies. Sauté for 30 seconds until fragrant, ensuring the garlic does not burn.
5. If using, add the sliced onion and sauté until translucent (about 2 minutes).
6. Introduce the sliced bamboo shoots and the turmeric powder to the wok. Stir-fry vigorously for 3-4 minutes to coat the bamboo shoots in the oil and aromatics.
7. Add the prepared Berma (dried fish/paste) and mix well, ensuring it disperses throughout the bamboo shoots. Cook for 1 minute.
8. Add 1/2 cup of water and a pinch of salt. Bring the mixture to a simmer, then cover the wok and allow the Chikhvi to cook for 8-10 minutes, or until the bamboo shoots are tender and most of the liquid has evaporated, resulting in a dry stir-fry consistency.
9. Taste and adjust the salt if necessary. Serve hot, garnished with fresh coriander leaves, alongside steamed rice.

title: Pahadi Aloo Tamaatar Tarkari (Sikkimese Potato Tomato Curry)
ingredients:
3 large Potatoes (Aloo), boiled, peeled, and cubed
4 medium Tomatoes, pureed or finely chopped
1 large Onion, finely chopped
1 tbsp Ginger-Garlic paste (or 1 tsp minced ginger, 4 cloves garlic minced)
2-3 Green Chilies (adjust to heat preference), slit lengthwise
3 tbsp Mustard Oil (Sarson ka Tel) or vegetable oil
1 tsp Cumin Seeds (Jeera)
1/4 tsp Asafoetida (Hing)
1 tsp Turmeric Powder (Haldi)
1.5 tsp Coriander Powder (Dhania)
1 tsp Cumin Powder (Jeera)
1/2 tsp Garam Masala (optional)
Salt to taste
1.5 - 2 cups Water
Fresh Coriander leaves (Dhaniya), chopped, for garnish
instructions:
1. Heat mustard oil in a heavy-bottomed pan or kadhai until smoking, then lower the heat slightly. Add cumin seeds and asafoetida; let them splutter.
2. Add the chopped onions and sauté over medium heat until they turn translucent and light golden brown.
3. Add the ginger-garlic paste (or minced aromatics) and green chilies. Sauté for 1-2 minutes until the raw smell disappears.
4. Pour in the tomato puree. Cook the tomatoes thoroughly, stirring occasionally, until the oil begins to separate from the masala (about 5-7 minutes).
5. Reduce the heat and add turmeric powder, coriander powder, cumin powder, and salt. Stir well and cook the dry spices for 1 minute.
6. Add the cubed boiled potatoes to the masala. Gently mix them so they are well coated with the spices.
7. Pour in the water (adjusting quantity for desired consistency—Sikkimese curries are often medium-thick). Bring the curry to a rolling boil.
8. Once boiling, reduce the heat, cover the pan, and simmer for 5 to 8 minutes, allowing the potatoes to absorb the flavors.
9. Stir in the Garam Masala (if using). Taste and adjust salt if necessary.
10. Garnish generously with freshly chopped coriander leaves and serve hot with rotis, parathas, or steamed rice.
